summary: The Gemara raises several dilemmas about the kometz: what if a meal-offering prepared in a pan is offered? What if the kometz is taken from bein habeinayim (the space between the fingers)? What if it is extended, pressed, or spread? And what if the High Priest scooped the handfuls at midnight?

display.whats: What is the law regarding a meal-offering prepared in a pan?
display.hint: The Gemara opens by noting a ruling about a meal-offering prepared in a pan (machavat): how is the kometz taken from it when the offering is in liquid/semi-liquid form? The rules differ from dry flour.
display.title: Kometz From a Pan-Offering: Same Measure, Different Texture

learning.ahaMoment: Pan meal-offering: the texture changes the kometz procedure - the three-finger rule still applies but the physical mechanics differ.
learning.memoryAnchor: Pan meal-offering requires kometz but the consistency makes it different from dry flour.
learning.learnerQuestion: Does the three-finger kometz measure still apply when the meal-offering is prepared in a pan rather than as dry flour?
learning.coreTension: A pan-prepared meal-offering (machavat) has a different, more semi-liquid consistency than loose dry flour, raising the question of whether the same kometz mechanics and measure can even apply to it.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara rules that the same three-finger kometz amount is taken from a pan-offering, with the physical technique adapting to the different consistency rather than the measure itself changing.

takeaway.text: The kometz from a pan-prepared meal-offering (machavat) is still the three-finger measure - the consistency changes the physical technique, not the required amount.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume a different-consistency offering requires a different kometz quantity, missing that the Gemara holds the measure constant and adapts only the technique.

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A meal-offering prepared in a pan - the kometz is taken in the same manner but with adapted technique for the consistency.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: A meal-offering prepared in a pan -

misconceptions.misconception: Because a pan-prepared meal-offering has a different consistency than dry flour, it requires a different kometz quantity than the standard three-finger measure.
misconceptions.correction: The kometz quantity stays the same three-finger measure regardless of consistency - only the physical technique adapts to the semi-liquid texture of a pan-prepared offering.

visualizableElements.description: Pan meal-offering vs standard meal-offering - two vessel types, different kometz mechanics

quizSeeds.answer: The same three-finger amount is taken, but the technique adapts to the semi-liquid consistency of the pan-prepared offering.
quizSeeds.question: How is kometz taken from a meal-offering prepared in a pan?

sugyot.finalRuling: Kometz from a pan meal-offering uses the same measure but adapted technique.

display.whats: What is the law if the kometz was taken 'bein habeinayim' (from the space between the fingers)?
display.hint: R. Yehoshua ben Uza'a raised the dilemma: what if incense was taken from the space between the fingers rather than by cupping the fingers together? This is a bein habeinayim dilemma - the flour/incense sits in the gaps between fingers rather than on the pads.
display.title: Between the Fingers: Does It Count?

learning.ahaMoment: Bein habeinayim: the flour is in the spaces between fingers, not on the pads. Valid kometz? The Gemara builds a case by analogy to other 'in-between' scenarios.
learning.memoryAnchor: If the offering settled between the fingers instead of on the pads - valid or not? The answer depends on what 'handfuls' means physically.
learning.learnerQuestion: If incense or flour settles in the gaps between the fingers rather than on the finger-pads, is that still a valid kometz?
learning.coreTension: A natural cupped grip leaves some material on the finger-pads and some in the gaps between fingers, and R. Yehoshua ben Uza'a's dilemma asks whether material resting only in those inter-finger gaps - not the pads - still counts as being 'in his hand.'
learning.coreMove: The Gemara narrows the dilemma specifically to material resting only between the fingers (since pad-contact is agreed to be valid) and weighs analogies for and against counting the inter-finger space as part of 'his handfuls,' leaving the question unresolved.

takeaway.text: Whether incense or flour resting only in the spaces between the fingers (bein habeinayim), rather than on the finger-pads, constitutes a valid kometz is a dilemma the Gemara raises and leaves unresolved.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume any flour touching any part of the hand counts as a valid kometz, missing that the Gemara specifically isolates the inter-finger gaps as a distinct, unresolved boundary case from the agreed-valid finger-pads.

argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yehoshua ben Uza'a
argumentFlow.text: What if the incense was taken from bein habeinayim (space between fingers)?
argumentFlow.label: Question: What if the incense was taken from

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The flour between the fingers - is it considered 'in his hand' or 'outside his hand'?
argumentFlow.label: Analysis: The flour between the fingers - is

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Let the dilemma be about the flour between the fingers specifically - is that location 'his handfuls' or not?
argumentFlow.label: Resolution: Let the dilemma be about the flour

misconceptions.misconception: Any incense or flour touching any part of the High Priest's cupped hand automatically counts as a valid kometz, with no distinction between locations.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara distinguishes the finger-pads (agreed valid) from the spaces between the fingers (bein habeinayim) - whether material resting only in those gaps counts as a valid kometz is raised as a specific, unresolved dilemma.

visualizableElements.label: bein habeinayim - flour in inter-finger spaces vs on fingerpads

quizSeeds.answer: Whether incense or flour that fell into the spaces between the fingers (rather than cupped on the finger-pads) constitutes a valid kometz.
quizSeeds.question: What is the 'bein habeinayim' dilemma in kometz law?

sugyot.finalRuling: Whether flour taken from between the fingers (bein habeinayim) constitutes a valid kometz remains an unresolved dilemma.

display.whats: What if the kometz was extended, pressed flat, or spread out?
display.hint: The Gemara raises further kometz dilemmas: what if the incense was extended (pashat) - spread beyond the finger boundaries? What if it was pressed (machatz) flat? What if it was spread wide (pizer)? Each represents a different way the kometz geometry might be altered.
display.title: Stretched, Pressed, or Scattered: Three Ways to Break the Kometz

learning.ahaMoment: Pashat, machatz, pizer: three ways the kometz shape might be altered. Each is a separate dilemma about whether the exact kometz requirement is still met.
learning.memoryAnchor: The mnemonic 'bein habeinayim, pashat, machatz, pizer, v'chishev' lists all the kometz geometry dilemmas.
learning.learnerQuestion: If the kometz is extended beyond the natural finger boundaries, pressed flat, or spread out, does it still count as a valid 'handful'?
learning.coreTension: The kometz's validity depends on a specific natural cupped shape, but the Gemara raises three distinct ways that shape might be altered - extended (pashat), pressed flat (machatz), or spread wide (pizer) - each testing whether 'his handfuls' still applies once the geometry changes.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara raises all three geometry dilemmas in turn, analyzing whether each altered shape still satisfies 'his handfuls,' and leaves all three unresolved as boundary cases defining the outer limits of the kometz requirement.

takeaway.text: Whether an extended (pashat), pressed-flat (machatz), or spread-out (pizer) kometz still satisfies 'his handfuls' are three distinct dilemmas the Gemara raises and leaves unresolved.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume these are minor variations of the same single question, missing that the Gemara treats pashat, machatz, and pizer as three distinct geometry dilemmas, each tested separately.

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Pashat (extended), machatz (pressed), pizer (spread) - three kometz geometry dilemmas.
argumentFlow.label: Question: Pashat (extended), machatz (pressed), pizer (spread) -

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Each alters the physical shape of what is in the hand - does 'his handfuls' require the natural cupped shape?
argumentFlow.label: Analysis: Each alters the physical shape of what

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: These dilemmas remain unresolved - they are boundary cases for the kometz requirement.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: These dilemmas remain unresolved - they are

misconceptions.misconception: Pashat, machatz, and pizer are essentially the same question about altering the kometz, raised once and not as three separate dilemmas.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara treats pashat (extended), machatz (pressed flat), and pizer (spread wide) as three distinct geometry dilemmas, each analyzed and left unresolved in its own right.

visualizableElements.label: three kometz geometry alterations: extended, pressed, spread

quizSeeds.answer: 'Extended' - when the kometz is spread or extended beyond the natural boundaries of the three fingers; a dilemma whether this invalidates the kometz.
quizSeeds.question: What is 'pashat' in the context of kometz law?

sugyot.finalRuling: Kometz geometry dilemmas (pashat, machatz, pizer) remain unresolved.

display.whats: What if the High Priest scooped the handfuls at midnight?
display.hint: Another kometz dilemma: the Yom Kippur incense service should be done during the day. If the High Priest scooped the handfuls at midnight (bein hashemashot or before dawn), is the kometz valid?
display.title: A Midnight Kometz: Can Daylight Validate It Retroactively?

learning.ahaMoment: Midnight kometz: taken before the permitted time. The question is whether daylight validates a pre-dawn kometz.
learning.memoryAnchor: If the kometz is taken at midnight, does it become valid when day comes?
learning.learnerQuestion: If the High Priest scoops the incense handfuls at midnight, before the Yom Kippur service may begin, is the kometz valid once day arrives?
learning.coreTension: The Yom Kippur incense service must occur by day, but the Gemara raises the dilemma of a kometz taken too early - at midnight - asking whether the act is invalid from the moment it happens or whether the arrival of daylight can retroactively validate it.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara raises the timing dilemma and treats it on the model of other Temple acts performed before their permitted time, which cannot be retroactively validated once the proper time arrives - so a kometz taken before the permitted time is invalid from the outset.

takeaway.text: Taking the kometz at midnight, before the Yom Kippur incense service may begin, is invalid - like other Temple acts performed before their permitted time, the later arrival of the proper time does not retroactively validate it.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may assume an act performed too early simply waits to 'become' valid once the proper time arrives, missing that the Gemara treats premature Temple acts as invalid from the outset, not retroactively curable.

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: What if he scooped the handfuls at midnight? Is the kometz valid?
argumentFlow.label: Question: What if he scooped the handfuls at

halachic: kometz timing - must be by day

misconceptions.misconception: If the High Priest takes the kometz too early, at midnight, the act becomes valid retroactively once daylight - the permitted time - arrives.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara treats a kometz taken before its permitted time as invalid from the outset, following the pattern of other Temple acts performed too early - the later arrival of the proper time does not retroactively validate it.

visualizableElements.description: Midnight kometz vs permitted dawn: sequence of when scooping must occur

quizSeeds.answer: The Yom Kippur incense service must be performed by day. If the High Priest took the kometz at midnight, it may be invalid; the question is whether daylight retrospectively validates it.
quizSeeds.question: What is the dilemma about taking the kometz at midnight?

sugyot.finalRuling: Taking the kometz at midnight is invalid; all Yom Kippur incense service must be during the day.

glossary.en: Between - literally 'between the betweens'; the space between the fingers. A dilemma whether incense in this space counts as a valid kometz.
Extended/spread - one of the kometz geometry dilemmas where the handful is extended beyond the natural finger boundaries.
A flat pan for preparing meal-offerings; kometz from pan-prepared offerings has different physical mechanics.

rashiTranslations.en: A meal-offering prepared in a pan - the consistency is different from loose flour.
The kometz from a machavat is still three fingers but the semi-solid texture changes the technique.
There is a separate discussion about whether pan-baked offerings are considered the same category.
R. Yehoshua ben Uza'a raised the dilemma about bein habeinayim.
Bein habeinayim: the flour or incense is in the spaces between the fingers.
Is the space between fingers considered 'in his hand' or 'outside his hand'?
The natural cupped position has flour both on the pads and between the fingers.
If flour only sits in the gaps (not on the pads), is that a valid kometz?
The Gemara: let the dilemma be about the inter-finger space specifically.
Not about the pads - everyone agrees flour on the pads is valid. Only the gaps are questionable.
Analogy: in other Temple contexts, 'in the hand' sometimes includes adjacent spaces.
Counter: 'his handfuls' implies the active gripping surfaces - the pads - not passive gaps.
The Gemara leaves this unresolved: teiku.
Another dilemma: what if he extended (pashat) the kometz - spread the fingers wider than normal?
Extending the fingers spreads the flour across a wider surface - more area, same three fingers.
Is this still 'his handfuls' or has the geometry been altered beyond what 'handfuls' means?
Third dilemma: machatz - pressing the kometz flat. The volume is the same but the shape changes.
Pressing flat could mean the incense extends beyond the finger-pads in a thin layer.
Fourth dilemma: pizer - dispersing or spreading the incense across the palm.
Pizer implies the incense is not tightly cupped but loosely distributed.
Fifth dilemma: v'chishev - thinking about the kometz (intent dilemma) while performing it.
The mnemonic: bein habeinayim, pashat, machatz, pizer, v'chishev covers all five dilemmas.
The Gemara raises all five but resolves none definitively - all are left as teiku.
Teiku: an unresolved Talmudic question. Acronym or ancient word, the question stands.
These boundary cases define the outer limits of what 'his handfuls' includes.
Practical importance: a kohen who asked 'what if' questions needed halakhic guidance.
Though Talmudic discussion doesn't resolve these, later poskim addressed them.
The Rambam rules that the kometz must be exact - deviations generally invalidate.
But the exact shape requirement is subject to debate precisely because these cases are left unresolved.
Additional dilemma: the handfuls scooped at midnight (chavat) - before the permitted time.
The Yom Kippur incense is a daytime act - nighttime kometz cannot precede the daytime service.
If scooped at midnight, the pre-dawn act cannot be validated retroactively when day comes.
This parallels other Temple laws where timing is part of the validity conditions.
A korban brought before its time cannot be retroactively validated when the time arrives.
Similarly, a nighttime kometz cannot be validated by daytime.
The Gemara: but what about an act done bein hashemashot (twilight) - partly valid?
Twilight is uncertain: if night, the kometz is invalid; if day, it might be valid.
This uncertainty creates a new dilemma for twilight-taken kometz.
The resolution: since the time is uncertain and the act requires certainty, it is invalid.
The Gemara now transitions to the next set of dilemmas about kometz on the following page.
47b is a page of unresolved dilemmas - this is intentional legal thinking.
The Talmud preserves the questions because the boundaries matter even without definitive answers.
By knowing where the questions are, later decisors can work outward from the known cases.
The kometz dilemmas of 47b represent the outer frontier of Temple law precision.
All the cases are 'what if' - not actual occurrences but theoretical boundary markers.
This style of Talmudic reasoning is called 'ha-matzui veha-ifshari' - what happens and what could happen.
The theoretical questions train the mind to understand the principle precisely.
Understanding the boundary of 'his handfuls' requires knowing all the edge cases.
47b exemplifies why Talmud study is called 'learning' even when no practical ruling is reached.
The intellectual exercise of defining the kometz boundary is itself the learning.
The mnemonic phrase helps students remember all five dilemmas in sequence.
Each term in the mnemonic corresponds to a different type of shape or timing deviation.
Students of Temple law were expected to know these dilemmas by heart.
47b concludes the first phase of kometz discussion; 48a will continue with Rav Pappa's dilemmas.
The transition from 47a (Kimhit's merit) to 47b (kometz dilemmas) is seamless.
Kimhit represents the standard being met perfectly; the dilemmas represent the edges of that standard.
Perfect kometz vs. edged kometz: the legal and the personal both define what 'perfect service' means.
47b leaves the student with five open questions - a productive discomfort.
The next page will add more dilemmas, deepening the uncertainty before resolution in later daf.
The Talmud is not troubled by unresolved questions; they are signposts of the boundary.
A question well-posed is half-answered - even if the other half awaits.
47b: five questions, zero resolutions, and a complete understanding of what's at stake.
That's the Talmud being the Talmud.
The reader arrives at 48a having thoroughly internalized the kometz concept's limits.
Perfect preparation for Rav Pappa's dilemmas which probe yet further.
